_note: Original Practice questions aligned with CS Navigator's original Learn lesson for this topic.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 1
questions.code:
a = Student("Ada", 12)
b = Student("Ben", 9)
b.credits += 3
print(a.credits, b.credits)
questions.explanation: The two calls create different objects. Changing b affects only b, so a stays at 12 while b becomes 12.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 2
questions.prompt: What must be the first parameter of this instance method?
questions.explanation: Python passes the receiving object into the first parameter. The usual name is self, and the method uses it to reach that object's value.

questions.title: Trace Classes & Objects
questions.prompt: What output does this code produce? Enter each line in order.
questions.explanation: `Student` is the class. Calling it creates one object, and `__init__` stores the values that belong to that object. The resulting output is 'Ada 18'.

questions.title: Classes & Objects practice 5
questions.prompt: In `__init__`, what is the difference between `self.name = name` and `name = name`?
choices: self.name stores it on the object; name = name only reassigns a local and is lost
They both store the value on the object
self.name creates a new class, not an attribute
questions.explanation: `self.name = name` attaches the value to the object, so other methods can read it later. `name = name` just assigns the parameter to itself, a local that vanishes when `__init__` returns, and the object never remembers the name. It runs without error but stores nothing.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 1
questions.prompt: What does the lesson mean by "Each object has its own data"?
choices: Creating a second Student does not copy or replace the first one. Each call to the class creates a separate object with separate instance attributes.
questions.explanation: A class describes the data and actions that its objects share. Create an object by calling the class, store its data on `self`, and use methods for work that belongs to that object. That reasoning supports the answer: Creating a second Student does not copy or replace the first one. Each call to the class creates a separate object with separate instance attributes.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 2
questions.prompt: Which choice describes a reliable Classes & Objects practice?
choices: Use a class when data and behavior belong together.
questions.explanation: A class describes the data and actions that its objects share. Create an object by calling the class, store its data on `self`, and use methods for work that belongs to that object. That reasoning supports the answer: Use a class when data and behavior belong together.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 3
questions.prompt: Which recommendation belongs in a review of Classes & Objects code?
questions.explanation: A class describes the data and actions that its objects share. Create an object by calling the class, store its data on `self`, and use methods for work that belongs to that object. That reasoning supports the answer: Set starting attributes inside `__init__`.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 4
questions.prompt: Which guideline helps prevent a common Classes & Objects mistake?
choices: Write `self.attribute` for data stored on an object.
questions.explanation: A class describes the data and actions that its objects share. Create an object by calling the class, store its data on `self`, and use methods for work that belongs to that object. That reasoning supports the answer: Write `self.attribute` for data stored on an object.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 5
questions.prompt: A classmate is checking their understanding of Classes & Objects. Which advice is correct?
choices: Remember that each class call creates a new object.
questions.explanation: A class describes the data and actions that its objects share. Create an object by calling the class, store its data on `self`, and use methods for work that belongs to that object. That reasoning supports the answer: Remember that each class call creates a new object.

questions.title: Classes & Objects concept check 6
questions.prompt: Which choice shows sound reasoning about Classes & Objects?
choices: Keep a method focused on one clear action.
questions.explanation: A class describes the data and actions that its objects share. Create an object by calling the class, store its data on `self`, and use methods for work that belongs to that object. That reasoning supports the answer: Keep a method focused on one clear action.
